en.extract:
Euler's flycatcher (Lathrotriccus euleri) is a small passerine bird in the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant flycatchers. It is found on Trinidad and in every mainland South American country except Chile. It formerly was found on Grenada.


Taxonomy and systematics
Euler's flycatcher has these five subspecies:

L. e. flaviventris (Lawrence, 1887)
L. e. lawrencei (Allen, JA, 1889)
L. e. bolivianus (Allen, JA, 1889)
L. e. argentinus (Cabanis, 1868)
L. e. euleri (Cabanis, 1868)
Euler's flycatcher has a complicated taxonomic history. What are now the subspecies L. e. euleri and L. e. argentinus were originally described in the same 1868 publication as Empidochanes euleri and Empidochanes argentinus respectively. Subspecies L. e. flaviventris was originally described as Blaccicus flaviventris. Subspecies L. e. lawrencei was originally described as Ochthaeca flaviventris but later renamed Empidonax lawrencei. In the early 1900s genus Empidochanes was merged into Empidonax. Some early twentieth century authors treated the northern subspecies L. e. flaviventris, L. e. lawrencei, and L. e. bolivianus as the separate species "Lawrence's flycatcher" but by mid-century all five taxa were generally regarded as subspecies of L. euleri. The Clements taxonomy subdivides the species into the "Lawrence's" and "Euler's" groups in recognition that the earlier treatment as two species might be valid.
Many authors retained Euler's flycatcher in Empidonax through most of the twentieth century. By the very early twenty-first century it was generally agreed to properly belong to Lathrotriccus which had been erected in 1986. During the several reclassifications some subspecies received new specific epithets to comply with the principle of priority.
Euler's flycatcher is named for the Swiss ornithologist Carl Euler. It shares genus Lathrotriccus with the gray-breasted flycatcher (L. griseipectus).


Description
Euler's flycatcher is 12.7 to 13.5 cm (5.0 to 5.3 in) long and weighs 9 to 13.8 g (0.32 to 0.49 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults of the nominate subspecies L. e. euleri have a warm olive-brown crown, nape, and cheeks. They have a whitish eye-ring and a faint pale stripe above the lores. Their upperparts are mostly olive-brown with a brown rump. Their wings are dusky with pale buff ends on the coverts that show as two wing bars. Their secondaries and tertials have pale buffy-brown edges. Their tail is dusky. Their throat is grayish white, their breast is washed with brownish olive, and their belly is pale yellow to white. 
The other subspecies of Euler's flycatcher differ from the nominate and each other thus:

L. e. flaviventris: similar to nominate
L. e. lawrencei: more olive upperparts than nominate, with whitish throat, drabber gray breast, and white belly
L. e. bolivianus: more olive upperparts than nominate but less so than lawrencei with pale olive-gray breast and a slightly less yellow belly than nominate
L. e. argentinus: drabber gray breast than nominate with whitish throat and white belly
All subspecies have a dark iris, a black maxilla, a dull pinkish or orangish pink mandible, and blackish brown legs and feet.


Distribution and habitat
The subspecies of Euler's flycatcher are found thus:

L. e. flaviventris: Grenada (possibly extinct)
L. e. lawrencei: Colombia's Eastern Andes, Andes and Coastal Ranges of western and northern Venezuela, Trinidad, and locally in the Guianas
L. e. bolivianus: Amazonas and Bolívar states in southern and eastern Venezuela south through eastern Ecuador and eastern Peru into northwestern Bolivia and east into much of Amazonian Brazil
L. e. argentinus: central and eastern Bolivia, northern and northeastern Argentina, and Paraguay; in migration eastern Peru and eastern Brazil
L. e. euleri: southeastern Brazil, northeastern Argentina, and Uruguay; in migration Peru, Bolivia, and Brazil
Euler's flycatcher inhabits moist to humid primary and secondary forest and woodlands in the tropical and lower subtropical zones. It favors the lower levels and edges of its habitat. In elevation it ranges from sea level to 1,500 m (4,900 ft) in Brazil. In Venezuela it is found below 1,600 m (5,200 ft) north of the Orinoco River and below 1,000 m (3,300 ft) south of it. It reaches 800 m (2,600 ft) in Colombia, 1,300 m (4,300 ft) in Ecuador, and 1,600 m (5,200 ft) in Peru.


Behavior


Movement
Most populations of Euler's flycatcher are year-round residents. Subspecies L. e. argentinus and the more southerly individuals of L. e. euleri move north for the austral winter into Peru and Brazil and perhaps as far as Venezuela. The species' migration timing, routes, and extent are barely known.


Feeding
Euler's flycatcher feeds on arthropods. It typically forages singly or in pairs and rarely to occasionally joins mixed-species feeding flocks. It perches upright in low vegetation, usually somewhat hidden, and captures prey in mid-air with sallies from the perch ("hawking"). It returns to the same perch or another one about equally.


Breeding
The breeding season of Euler's flycatcher varies geographically, spanning May to July on Trinidad, September to November in Brazil, and October to as late as January in Argentina. Its nest is an open cup made from plant fibers and lined with soft black fibers, built in a branch fork or perched on the edge of a hole in a trunk. It typically is placed within about 2 m (7 ft) of the ground but may be higher. The clutch is two or three eggs. They are white, marked with reddish brown mostly at the larger end, and in a study in Argentina weighed about 1.7 g (0.060 oz) and measured roughly 18 mm × 13.5 mm (0.71 in × 0.53 in). In that study only the female incubated, for 16 to 18 days, and fledging occurred about 15 days after hatch. Both parents provisioned the nestlings. In the study area predation was found to be moderate to light.


Vocalization
In Brazil Euler's flycatcher sings a "varied series of 2-5 notes, 1st one normally stressed and slightly longer than slightly descending notes; thereafter Tjeew-tjuh or Tjeew-terduh". In Venezuela its song is described as "an abrupt, almost explosive FEEU! pe'pe 'pe'p'p, 1st note loudest, last notes run together, buzzy and downscale". In Peru it is described as "a repeated, 2-note, buzzy, descending DZEER-hur (hur sometimes lacking) interspersed with sneezing DZEER-wee'chew phrases". There its call is "a buzzy, descending series of 2-7, usually 4, notes DZEE-dzee-dzee-dzer or DZEEW-dzew-dzew-dzeer".


Status
The IUCN has assessed Euler's flycatcher as being of Last Concern. It has an extremely large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. However, subspecies L. e. flaviventris has not been recorded since 1950 and is possibly extinct. It is considered "uncommon to locally fairly common" in Venezuela, "rather uncommon and possibly seasonal" in Colombia, "scarce and local" in Ecuador, and "uncommon but widespread" in Peru. The species is found in many protected areas across its range.


References


Further reading
ffrench, Richard; O'Neill, John Patton & Eckelberry, Don R. (1991): A guide to the birds of Trinidad and Tobago (2nd edition). Comstock Publishing, Ithaca, N.Y. ISBN 0-8014-9792-2
Hilty, Steven L. (2003): Birds of Venezuela. Christopher Helm, London. ISBN 0-7136-6418-5

| status = LC

| status_ref = <ref name=IUCN>{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2024 |title=Euler's Flycatcher ''Lathrotriccus euleri'' |volume=2024 |article-number=e.T22699751A264375958 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2024-2.RLTS.T22699751A264375958.en |access-date=28 March 2025}}</ref>

'''Euler's flycatcher''' ('''''Lathrotriccus euleri''''') is a small [[passerine]] [[bird]] in the family [[Tyrannidae]], the tyrant flycatchers.<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flycatchers/ |title=Tyrant flycatchers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=March 3, 2025 }}</ref> It is found on [[Trinidad]] and in every mainland [[South America]]n country except [[Chile]].<ref name=SACCcountries>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 30 January 2025. Species Lists of Birds for South American Countries and Territories. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCCountryLists.htm retrieved 31 January 2025</ref> It formerly was found on [[Grenada]].<ref name=AOU1998>{{cite book | last =<!--Not stated--> | first =<!--Not stated--> |  title =Check-list of North American Birds | publisher =American Ornithologists' Union |edition =7th | date =1998 | location =Washington, D.C. | page =388 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

Euler's flycatcher has these five subspecies:<ref name=IOC15.1/>

*''L. e. flaviventris'' <small>([[George Newbold Lawrence|Lawrence]], 1887)</small>
*''L. e. lawrencei'' <small>([[Joel Asaph Allen|Allen, JA]], 1889)</small>
*''L. e. bolivianus'' <small>(Allen, JA, 1889)</small>
*''L. e. argentinus'' <small>([[Jean Cabanis|Cabanis]], 1868)</small>
*''L. e. euleri'' <small>(Cabanis, 1868)</small>

Euler's flycatcher has a complicated taxonomic history. What are now the subspecies ''L. e. euleri'' and ''L. e. argentinus'' were [[Species description|originally described]] in the same 1868 publication as ''Empidochanes euleri'' and ''Empidochanes argentinus'' respectively.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Cabanis |first=Jean |title=Neue Arten der südamerikanischen Gattung ''Empidochanes'', im Berliner Museum | date= 1853 | journal=Journal für Ornithologie |volume=XVI |number=93 |pages=195–196 |language=German | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/33009775 |access-date=March 28, 2025 }}</ref> Subspecies ''L. e. flaviventris'' was originally described as ''Blaccicus flaviventris''.<ref>{{cite journal | editor-last=Lawrence |editor-first=George N. |last=Wells |first=John Grant |title=A catalogue of the birds of Grenada, West Indies, with observations thereon | date= 1886 | journal=Proceedings of the United States National Museum | publisher=Smithsonian Institution Press, [etc.] | volume=IX |pages=617–618 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/7601349 |access-date=March 28, 2025}} The article's byline is given to John Grant Wells with Lawrence listed as editor. Wells collected the specimens and sent them to Lawrence for their formal description. The journal issue is for the year 1886 and was published in 1887.</ref> Subspecies ''L. e. lawrencei'' was originally described as ''Ochthaeca flaviventris'' but later renamed ''Empidonax lawrencei''.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Allen |first=J. A. |date=1889 |title=Descriptions of New Species of South American Birds, with Remarks on various other little known Species |url=https://digitallibrary.amnh.org/items/7986edfb-79e9-440a-bc80-ef2c499d36fb |journal=Bulletin of the AMNH |volume=2 |pages=150–151 |access-date=March 28, 2025 }}</ref> In the early 1900s genus ''Empidochanes'' was merged into ''[[Empidonax]]''. Some early twentieth century authors treated the northern subspecies ''L. e. flaviventris'', ''L. e. lawrencei'', and ''L. e. bolivianus'' as the separate species "Lawrence's flycatcher" but by mid-century all five taxa were generally regarded as subspecies of ''L. euleri''.<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 30 January 2025. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved 31 January 2025</ref><ref name=EUFL-BOW>Farnsworth, A. and D. J. Lebbin (2020). Euler's Flycatcher (''Lathrotriccus euleri''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.eulfly1.01 retrieved March 28, 2025</ref> The [[Clements taxonomy]] subdivides the species into the "Lawrence's" and "Euler's" groups in recognition that the earlier treatment as two species might be valid.<ref name=AOU1998/><ref name=Clements2024>Clements, J. F., P.C. Rasmussen, T. S. Schulenberg, M. J. Iliff, T. A. Fredericks, J. A. Gerbracht, D. Lepage, A. Spencer, S. M. Billerman, B. L. Sullivan, M. Smith, and C. L. Wood. 2024. The eBird/Clements checklist of birds of the world: v2024. Downloaded from https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ retrieved October 23, 2024</ref>

Many authors retained Euler's flycatcher in ''Empidonax'' through most of the twentieth century. By the very early twenty-first century it was generally agreed to properly belong to ''Lathrotriccus'' which had been erected in 1986. During the several reclassifications some subspecies received new [[specific epithet]]s to comply with the [[principle of priority]].<ref name=SACClist/><ref name=EUFL-BOW/><ref>{{cite journal |journal=[[The Auk]] |title=Generic Status of Euler's Flycatcher:A Morphological and Biochemical Study |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v103n02/p0341-p0350.pdf |volume=103 |issue=2 |pages=341–350 |last1=Lanyon |first1=Wesley E |last2=Lanyon |first2=Scott M|year=1986 |doi=10.1093/auk/103.2.341 |access-date=March 28, 2025 }}</ref>

Euler's flycatcher is named for the Swiss [[ornithologist]] [[Carl Euler]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Beolens|first=Bo|title=Whose Birds? Men and Women Commemorated in the Common Names of Birds|year=2003|publisher=Christopher Helm|location=London|page=121|author2=Watkins, Michael}}</ref> It shares genus ''Lathrotriccus'' with the [[gray-breasted flycatcher]] (''L. griseipectus'').<ref name=IOC15.1/>

==Description==

Euler's flycatcher is {{convert|12.7|to|13.5|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|9|to|13.8|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults of the [[nominate subspecies]] ''L. e. euleri'' have a warm olive-brown crown, nape, and cheeks. They have a whitish [[eye-ring]] and a faint pale stripe above the [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]]. Their upperparts are mostly olive-brown with a brown rump. Their wings are dusky with pale buff ends on the [[coverts]] that show as two [[wing bar]]s. Their [[Secondaries (birds)|secondaries]] and [[tertials]] have pale buffy-brown edges. Their tail is dusky. Their throat is grayish white, their breast is washed with brownish olive, and their belly is pale yellow to white.<ref name=EUFL-BOW/><ref name=vanPerlo>{{cite book | last =van Perlo | first = Ber| title =A Field Guide to the Birds of Brazil | publisher =Oxford University Press | date =2009 | location =New York | pages =312–313 | isbn =978-0-19-530155-7 }}</ref><ref name=Peña>{{cite book | last =de la Peña | first =Martín R. | last2=Rumboll |first2=Maurice|  title =Birds of Southern South America and Antarctica | publisher =Princeton University Press | series =Princeton Illustrated Checklists | date =2001 | location =New Jersey | pages =Plate 74, map 74.2 | isbn =0-691-09035-1 }}</ref> 

The other subspecies of Euler's flycatcher differ from the nominate and each other thus:<ref name=EUFL-BOW/>

*''L. e. flaviventris'': similar to nominate
*''L. e. lawrencei'': more olive upperparts than nominate, with whitish throat, drabber gray breast, and white belly<ref name=Hilty>{{cite book | last =Hilty | first =Steven L. | title =Birds of Venezuela | publisher =Princeton University Press | edition =second | date =2003 | location =Princeton NJ | page =611 | language =English }}</ref>
*''L. e. bolivianus'': more olive upperparts than nominate but less so than ''lawrencei'' with pale olive-gray breast and a slightly less yellow belly than nominate<ref name=Hilty/><ref name=Ridgely2>{{cite book | last =Ridgely | first =Robert S. | last2 =Greenfield | first2 =Paul J. | title =The Birds of Ecuador: Field Guide | publisher =Cornell University Press | volume = II| date =2001 | location =Ithaca | page =505 | isbn =978-0-8014-8721-7 }}</ref><ref name=Schulenberg>{{cite book | last =Schulenberg | first =T.S. | last2 =Stotz | first2 =D.F. | last3 =Lane | first3 =D.F. | last4 =O'Neill | first4 =J.P. | last5 =Parker | first5 =T.A. III | title =Birds of Peru | publisher =Princeton University Press | edition =revised and updated |series=Princeton Field Guides | date =2010 | location =Princeton, NJ | page =446  |isbn = 978-0-691-13023-1 }}</ref>
*''L. e. argentinus'': drabber gray breast than nominate with whitish throat and white belly<ref name=Peña/><ref name=Schulenberg/>

All subspecies have a dark iris, a black [[maxilla]], a dull pinkish or orangish pink [[mandible]], and blackish brown legs and feet.<ref name=EUFL-BOW/>

==Distribution and habitat==

The subspecies of Euler's flycatcher are found thus:<ref name=EUFL-BOW/>

*''L. e. flaviventris'': Grenada (possibly extinct)<ref name=AOU1998/>
*''L. e. lawrencei'': Colombia's [[Cordillera Oriental (Colombia)|Eastern Andes]], Andes and [[Venezuelan Coastal Range|Coastal Ranges]] of western and northern Venezuela, Trinidad, and locally in [[the Guianas]]<ref name=Hilty/><ref name=McMullan>{{cite book | last1 =McMullan | first1 =Miles | last2 =Donegan | first2 =Thomas M. | last3 =Quevedo | first3 =Alonso |  title = Field Guide to the Birds of Colombia | publisher = Fundación ProAves| date =2010 | location =Bogotá | page =159  |isbn =978-0-9827615-0-2 }}</ref>
*''L. e. bolivianus'': [[Amazonas (Venezuelan state)|Amazonas]] and [[Bolívar (state)|Bolívar]] states in southern and eastern Venezuela south through eastern Ecuador and eastern Peru into northwestern Bolivia and east into much of Amazonian Brazil<ref name=Hilty/><ref name=Ridgely2/><ref name=Schulenberg/>
*''L. e. argentinus'': central and eastern Bolivia, northern and northeastern Argentina, and Paraguay; in migration eastern Peru and eastern Brazil<ref name=vanPerlo/><ref name=Peña/><ref name=Schulenberg/>
*''L. e. euleri'': southeastern Brazil, northeastern Argentina, and Uruguay; in migration Peru, Bolivia, and Brazil<ref name=vanPerlo/><ref name=Peña/><ref name=Schulenberg/>

Euler's flycatcher inhabits moist to humid [[Primary forest|primary]] and [[secondary forest|secondary]] forest and woodlands in the tropical and lower subtropical zones. It favors the lower levels and edges of its habitat.<ref name=AOU1998/><ref name=EUFL-BOW/><ref name=vanPerlo/> In elevation it ranges from sea level to {{convert|1500|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Brazil.<ref name=vanPerlo/> In Venezuela it is found below {{convert|1600|m|ft|abbr=on}} north of the [[Orinoco River]] and below {{convert|1000|m|ft|abbr=on}} south of it.<ref name=Hilty/> It reaches {{convert|800|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Colombia, {{convert|1300|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Ecuador, and {{convert|1600|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Peru.<ref name=Ridgely2/><ref name=Schulenberg/><ref name=McMullan/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

Most populations of Euler's flycatcher are year-round residents. Subspecies ''L. e. argentinus'' and the more southerly individuals of ''L. e. euleri'' move north for the [[South|austral]] winter into Peru and Brazil and perhaps as far as Venezuela. The species' migration timing, routes, and extent are barely known.<ref name=AOU1998/><ref name=EUFL-BOW/><ref name=vanPerlo/><ref name=Hilty/><ref name=Schulenberg/>

===Feeding===

Euler's flycatcher feeds on [[arthropod]]s. It typically forages singly or in pairs and rarely to occasionally joins [[mixed-species feeding flock]]s. It perches upright in low vegetation, usually somewhat hidden, and captures prey in mid-air with sallies from the perch ([[Hawking (birds)|"hawking"]]). It returns to the same perch or another one about equally.<ref name=EUFL-BOW/><ref name=Hilty/><ref name=Ridgely2/><ref name=McMullan/><ref>{{cite journal |author1=Vagner de A. Gabriel |author2=Marco A. Pizo |year=2005 |title=Foraging behavior of tyrant flycatchers (Aves, Tyrannidae) in Brazil |journal=[[Revista Brasileira de Zoologia]] |volume=22 |issue=4 |pages=1072–1077 |doi=10.1590/S0101-81752005000400036 |url=http://www.scielo.br/pdf/rbzool/v22n4/a36v22n4.pdf|doi-access=free }}</ref>

===Breeding===

The breeding season of Euler's flycatcher varies geographically, spanning May to July on Trinidad, September to November in Brazil, and October to as late as January in Argentina. Its nest is an open cup made from plant fibers and lined with soft black fibers, built in a branch fork or perched on the edge of a hole in a trunk. It typically is placed within about {{convert|2|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} of the ground but may be higher. The clutch is two or three eggs.<ref name=EUFL-BOW/><ref name=Hilty/> They are white, marked with reddish brown mostly at the larger end, and in a study in Argentina weighed about {{convert|1.7|g|oz|abbr=on}} and measured roughly {{convert|18|x|13.5|mm|in|abbr=on}}. In that study only the female incubated, for 16 to 18 days, and fledging occurred about 15 days after hatch. Both parents provisioned the nestlings. In the study area [[predator|predation]] was found to be moderate to light.<ref name=aueretal2007>{{cite journal |author=Sonya K. Auer |author2=Ronald D. Bassar |author3=Joseph J. |author4=Fontaine |author5=Thomas E. Martin |name-list-style=amp |year=2007 |title=Breeding biology of passerines in a subtropical montane forest in Northwestern Argentina |journal=[[The Condor (journal)|The Condor]] |volume=109 |issue=2 |pages=321–333 |doi=10.1650/0010-5422(2007)109[321:BBOPIA]2.0.CO;2 |url=http://www.umt.edu/mcwru/files.pdf/Tom's%20PDF/Auer%20et%20al%20%202007%20Condor%20Argentina%20breeding%20biology.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080408083929/http://www.umt.edu/mcwru/files.pdf/Tom%27s%20PDF/Auer%20et%20al%20%202007%20Condor%20Argentina%20breeding%20biology.pdf |archive-date=2008-04-08 }}</ref>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Lathrotriccus-euleri |species=Euler's flycatcher}}
===Vocalization===

In Brazil Euler's flycatcher sings a "varied series of 2-5 notes, 1st one normally stressed and slightly longer than slightly descending notes; thereafter ''Tjeew-tjuh'' or ''Tjeew-terduh''".<ref name=vanPerlo/> In Venezuela its song is described as "an abrupt, almost explosive ''FEEU! pe'pe 'pe'p'p'', 1st note loudest, last notes run together, buzzy and downscale".<ref name=Hilty/> In Peru it is described as "a repeated, 2-note, buzzy, descending ''DZEER-hur'' (''hur'' sometimes lacking) interspersed with sneezing ''DZEER-wee'chew'' phrases". There its call is "a buzzy, descending series of 2-7, usually 4, notes ''DZEE-dzee-dzee-dzer'' or ''DZEEW-dzew-dzew-dzeer''".<ref name=Schulenberg/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed Euler's flycatcher as being of Last Concern. It has an extremely large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. However, subspecies ''L. e. flaviventris'' has not been recorded since 1950 and is possibly extinct.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered "uncommon to locally fairly common" in Venezuela, "rather uncommon and possibly seasonal" in Colombia, "scarce and local" in Ecuador, and "uncommon but widespread" in Peru.<ref name=Hilty/><ref name=Ridgely2/><ref name=Schulenberg/><ref name=McMullan/> The species is found in many protected areas across its range.<ref name=EUFL-BOW/>
